answer:
# Adding a zod validator: how its type flows end-to-end

The atlas for Hono captures this exact pipeline in **ADR-04 "Type inference to typed client"** (`ADR-04-type-inference-to-typed-client.md`). It's described as a four-segment chain that must be preserved end-to-end:

> "The type-inference chain from route registration through handler Context to typed client must be preserved end-to-end across all four segments (route schema accumulation, path propagation, validator Input flow, client inference)." — ADR-04 (severity: hard)

Here's how the validator's type travels through each segment, with the symbols the atlas points at.

## 1. The validator produces an `Input`

When you wire a zod validator into a route, e.g.:

```ts
import { zValidator } from '@hono/zod-validator'
import { z } from 'zod'

const schema = z.object({ name: z.string(), age: z.number() })

const app = new Hono()
  .post('/users', zValidator('json', schema), (c) => {
    const body = c.req.valid('json')   // typed as { name: string; age: number }
    return c.json({ ok: true, body })
  })
```

…`zValidator` is a thin adapter on top of Hono's core `validator()` (`sym:ts:src/validator/validator.ts:validator`). The atlas calls out that the public type signature here is load-bearing:

> "Backwards-incompatible changes to `validator()`'s Input production belong in major version bumps with migration guidance. Third-party validator adapters depend on the `validator()` type signature and breaking it cascades across the ecosystem." — ADR-04 (severity: soft)

The validator's role in the type system is to produce an `Input` (`sym:ts:src/types.ts:Input`) describing both the *target* (`'json' | 'form' | 'query' | 'param' | 'header' | 'cookie'`) and the in/out shapes derived from your zod schema.

## 2. `Input` merges into the handler's `Context`

That `Input` is then merged into the next handler in the chain via the `MiddlewareHandler` generics:

> "The validator's `Input` must merge into the downstream handler's Context so `c.req.valid(target)` returns the validated type." — ADR-04 (severity: hard, excerpt: *"the validator's `Input` merges into the downstream handler's `Context<E, P, I>` so `c.req.valid(target)` returns the validated type."*)

Concretely:

- `MiddlewareHandler` (`sym:ts:src/types.ts:MiddlewareHandler`) carries `I` (the accumulated `Input`) forward.
- `Context<E, P, I>` (`sym:ts:src/context.ts:Context`) consumes it so that `c.req.valid('json')` is typed as the zod-inferred output, not `any`.
- Path generics travel the same way: `MergePath<BasePath, P>` (`sym:ts:src/types.ts:MergePath`) ensures `c.req.param('name')` is typed from the route string, even through `.basePath()` / `.route()` mounts.

The atlas explicitly warns middleware authors not to break this:

> "Middleware authors modifying `MiddlewareHandler` generics must preserve `Input` propagation. Seemingly local changes to generic defaults or parameter order can silently break validator→handler inference for all users." — ADR-04 (severity: hard)

## 3. Route registration accumulates it into the app's `Schema`

When the route returns, the `Hono` instance (`sym:ts:src/hono-base.ts:Hono`) re-types itself with a wider `Schema` generic:

> "Route registration must return a HonoBase whose Schema accumulates losslessly as `S & ToSchema<M, P, I, MergeTypedResponse<R>>`." — ADR-04 (severity: hard)

So your `I` (validated input) and `R` (handler return) get baked into `Schema` (`sym:ts:src/types.ts:Schema`) via `ToSchema` (`sym:ts:src/types.ts:ToSchema`). Chaining `app.post(...).get(...).route(...)` keeps accumulating without loss.

This is also why every overload in `HandlerInterface` (`sym:ts:src/types.ts:HandlerInterface`) has to be kept in lockstep:

> "Each route method must be typed via `HandlerInterface` with overloads for every handler-chain arity… adding route-level features requires extending overloads for every arity." — ADR-04 (severity: hard)

If only the single-handler overload is updated, your three-arg `.post(path, zValidator(...), handler)` form silently collapses to `any`.

## 4. `hc<typeof app>` reads `Schema` to produce the typed RPC client

Finally, on the client side:

```ts
import { hc } from 'hono/client'
import type app from './server'

const client = hc<typeof app>('http://localhost:3000')

// .$post is typed: body must satisfy the zod input shape
const res = await client.users.$post({ json: { name: 'Ada', age: 36 } })
const data = await res.json()  // typed from the handler's return
```

`hc` (`sym:ts:src/client/client.ts:hc`, with `Client` at `sym:ts:src/client/types.ts:Client`, and the Proxy machinery at `sym:ts:src/client/client.ts:createProxy`) walks `typeof app`'s accumulated `Schema` and synthesizes per-route methods. The validator's input becomes the required request body type; the handler's return becomes the typed response.

ADR-04 explicitly accepts the consequence:

> "Response-shape changes are accepted as compile-time breaking changes for all typed `hc` clients. `hc<typeof app>` makes response-shape changes compile-time breaking changes for every `hc` consumer." — ADR-04 (severity: context)

And rejects code-gen alternatives (OpenAPI codegen, runtime introspection, explicit annotations) as drift- or duplication-prone — the validator → handler → client inference is intended to be the single source of truth.

## TL;DR diagram

```
zValidator('json', zSchema)
        │  produces Input I = { in: { json: ... }, out: { json: ... } }
        ▼
MiddlewareHandler<E, P, I>      ── src/types.ts:MiddlewareHandler
        │  I propagates onto next handler
        ▼
Context<E, P, I>                ── src/context.ts:Context
   c.req.valid('json') : I['out']['json']
        │  handler returns R
        ▼
Hono.post() returns Hono<E, S & ToSchema<'post', P, I, R>, BasePath>
        │  S accumulates losslessly
        ▼
hc<typeof app>()                ── src/client/client.ts:hc
   client.users.$post({ json: ... })  ← request body typed from I
   res.json()                          ← response typed from R
```

The single rule to remember from ADR-04: **don't drop `I` anywhere in that pipeline.** Silent degradation to `any` is called out as the worst failure mode because it compiles cleanly while quietly losing autocomplete and type safety on every downstream consumer.
